title: Chapter Six: What a Lame Chord Sounds Like

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Chapter Six: What a Lame Chord Sounds Like

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The registry hall smelled of chalk dust and old paper and the damp that seeped from stone walls that never fully dried. The registrar who met him had a copper guild pin on her collar and the focused stillness of someone who had answered the same questions for twenty years.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Bellwright,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said, writing it down.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: First name of the contracting party?

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: Perin. P-E-R-I-N.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Contracting with?

segments.speaker: PERIN
segments.text: The House of Corda.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She went to the index cabinet, pulled a drawer, ran her finger along a row of cards. Pulled one. Looked at it. Looked at him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: How old are you?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Fourteen.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And your relation?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Brother.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She set the card on the counter between them, face down.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: I'm sorry,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said, and she did sound it, a little.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The record is sealed under privacy clause 7-C. Standard for service contracts involving minors at the time of signing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Perin had been sixteen. A minor, technically. Just barely.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Can I request a review?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: You can. You'd need a licensed song-broker to file the petition. The fee is forty-three copper, and the review timeline is six to eight weeks from filing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Forty-three copper. He had eleven, in the tin box under his bed.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Thank you,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said. She nodded and returned the card to the drawer. The small metallic click of it closing was the cleanest sound in the room.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was sitting on the worn lip of the Academy's dry fountain when Lenne found him, twenty minutes later, notebook already open, writing something in the margin with her left hand.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Registry,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said, without looking up.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Perin's contract?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Sealed. Privacy clause. Forty-three copper to file a petition, six to eight weeks.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Seven-C.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She closed the notebook.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: My mother filed one of those last year. Took nine weeks.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: That was not helpful. He didn't say so. She looked at him sideways.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Your interval's wrong.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He almost laughed — it came up unexpectedly and he swallowed most of it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: What does that mean.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: It means you're running through options and none of them are working and you look like someone handed you a chord that won't resolve. I've seen you in theory class when the interval's wrong. You make that face.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I don't make a face.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: You make a face.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Across the courtyard, a second-year student was practicing a ratification progression in the south arcade — a major third, held long and clean, two notes locking together the way two hands lock in a confirmed grip, the sound the Academy used to mean

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] yes, agreed, I am bound to this.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass's ear filed it away as correctly formed and moved on.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The sealed record blocks the petition. I need a broker to file, which costs money I don't have. And even if I had it, six weeks before anything comes back. But something's happening with the maintenance clause in Perin's contract — I think the timeline matters. I don't know why. I just think it does.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne was quiet in the way she had of checking her work before showing it.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Torvald Hess,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Who?

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Retired dye-merchant. He runs something in the Weavers' District.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: My mother mentioned him once. She was annoyed about it, which is how I remembered. He keeps tally-songs of contract disputes in the outer districts. Twenty years' worth, maybe more. She said he was thorough. She said it the way she says things she doesn't want to be true.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Is there any chance he'd have records on Corda?

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Corda has resonance chambers in the Weavers' District. They've been running contracts over there for years.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She picked up her notebook, not to write in it, just to have something in her hands.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: If anyone was keeping track of those disputes —

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He would have been.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: That's what I'm thinking.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at the dry fountain basin. Someone had dropped a copper quarter into it years ago and never retrieved it — he could see it down in the stone, green with age, dull. He wondered if it still rang at A when struck.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Anything he has isn't legally usable,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: No. But you're not trying to file a court case today. You're trying to understand what you're dealing with. Those are different problems.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: That was a cleaner way of putting it than he'd managed on his own. He sat with it for a moment, then stood.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Do you know where in the Weavers' District?

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Near the dye-vats on the western end.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She paused.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: That's all I have.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The Weavers' District hit him before he reached it — sulfur and indigo fermentation and something he'd never found a name for. Past the Fade Line the acoustic geometry loosened; a flat note was just a flat note. He asked twice for directions and got pointed to a building with a blue-stained doorframe, the color worked into the grain from decades of dye-stained hands.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The room had been a merchant's back office once. Shelves still held sample cards and empty pigment jars. But the center had been cleared for a long table, and on the table were strips of notched wood arranged in rows, weighted at the corners with smooth river-stones. Dozens, maybe more — each about the length of his forearm, the wood worn pale from handling. The notches along the top edge were cut at different depths and angles, in a system he didn't recognize. Small circles of wax pressed into the lower margin — witness seals, but not the Academy's copper guild seals. Something rougher. He picked one up and ran his thumb along the notches.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The man behind the table was large, going soft with age, with hands stained blue-green so permanently it looked like his natural color. He was cupping his left ear toward Cass in a way that was not a gesture, just how he held his head.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Close the door,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said. Cass closed it.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Bellwright boy.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He said it before Cass could speak.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I told you to come find me when you had credentials. You don't have credentials yet.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He gestured at the chair across the table.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Sit anyway.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass sat. He set the strip down, not wanting to be caught holding it.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I heard you were asking around,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I only asked two people.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Two people here is enough.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He picked up one of the strips, turned it in his blue-green hands.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: What do you need.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Records on the House of Corda. Service contracts specifically.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass paused.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I need to understand the structure of a specific contract. The original record is sealed at the Academy.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Whose contract.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: My brother's.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald set the strip down. He looked at Cass with the kind of attention that wasn't sympathy and wasn't calculation but something between the two — a man taking an accurate measurement.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Perin Bellwright,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Signed twelve years ago.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass went still.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You know it.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I know of it. Nobody outside Corda and the Academy registry has the contract itself.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He shifted in his chair.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: But I have tally-songs on four other Corda service contracts from the same period. And I've been watching the maintenance filing on your family's custodianship.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: It went in three days ago.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I know. We got the notice.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Then you know they're moving.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at Cass steadily.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Question is what you're planning to do about it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm trying to understand what I'm dealing with first.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Good answer.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald folded his hands on the table. He looked at the strip he'd set down, then back at Cass.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: You were at the Corda compound last autumn — don't look surprised, you were seen — and you stood outside the gate for a while. What did you hear?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at him. Torvald's expression didn't change.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The bells inside were running flat. Wrong for the district. A quarter-tone, maybe less. High register — the pitch range you'd use for codicil ratification. Amendment confirmation.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Which direction flat.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Down. They were pulling down from the ratification note.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald was quiet. He looked at Cass the way Cass's father looked at a bell he was trying to diagnose without striking it — gathering information before committing to an interpretation.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Tell me what else you've heard in any Corda context. Exact pitch. Forget the Academy words for it. Just the sound.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: So Cass told him about the courtroom the previous spring — a defendant's oath in G-major, coached and correct on the surface, and the thing underneath that had put a needle behind his left eye for the rest of the afternoon. He tried to describe it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Like someone shakes your hand but only squeezes with three fingers. You register the handshake. You register something's wrong. It holds one side without holding the other.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Yes,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said. He said it quietly, the way you say something you've been trying to find the right words for.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Yes, that's exactly it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He reached across the table and picked up a strip — not one of the ones in front of Cass, one from a separate pile he'd been keeping to his left. He turned it so Cass could see the notch-marks along the edge.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: This is what that looks like in a tally-song. The Academy calls it a lame chord. I've been calling it a trap interval for twenty years, but lame chord is better. A binding that grips one party without gripping the other.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set the strip down between them and put his finger on a specific notch.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: See this mark? Ratification interval. Major third — your handshake, both parties squeezing. Binding confirmed.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He moved his finger to a notch further along, angled differently, cut shallower.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: This is the codicil added in year two. See how the direction changes? The Academy notation would call this an ascending resolution. It should go down. It went up.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at Cass.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: One hand squeezing. The other hand thinking it shook.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at the two notches. The difference between them was, to him, invisible — the same depth, the same approximate angle, nothing his eye could catch. But he ran his thumb along them the way he'd done with the first strip, feeling for the difference, and there it was. The second notch was cut fractionally shallower on its right side. A lean. A lean in the wrong direction.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: And the person who signed it. Do they know?

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That's the question, isn't it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald took the strip back.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: See, if you're trained — and I mean properly trained, not the three-week course the Academy sells to traders — you might catch it. But most people aren't, are they? They're nervous, they're being rushed, the Court Singer sounds like he knows what he's about, and you trust the form because what else are you going to trust? It's like buying dyed cloth in bad light — the color looks right, the weave feels right, you take it home and the next morning you see it's not the shade you paid for. But you already cut it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That's what a lame chord does. You walk away bound to something you didn't agree to, wearing a color you didn't choose, and you spend years trying to work out why it sits wrong.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He rubbed his stained thumb along the strip's edge.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: And that's the thing that makes it complicated — she's not wrong that the outer districts needed someone who understood contracts. Before Corda, people out here got eaten alive by the brokers. Maret built something real. She just built it on a lie.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass pressed his tongue to the roof of his mouth.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Come back tomorrow,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I'll have the records pulled. The four Corda service contracts I mentioned, and what I have on the maintenance filings. You can look at them yourself.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Can you read folk notation?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: No.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I'll teach you enough to follow along. You read Academy notation?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Same intervals. Different marks.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He went back to sorting the tally-songs.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Go home. Get some sleep.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stood. He reached for the chair back, steadied himself.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Hess.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald looked up.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The maintenance filing. The access clause. Do your records show whether Corda has used it before? The acoustician access to the bells?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald looked at him for a moment with that same measuring attention.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That's exactly the right question,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Come back tomorrow and I'll tell you what I found.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He walked Cass toward the district's edge. The Fade Line wasn't marked. You knew it because you felt the air change — sound stopped being gathered by the limestone curve and became ordinary, undirected. Cass felt it as a loosening behind his ears.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A table was set up at the intersection where cobblestones gave way to packed earth. A song-broker — not Academy-licensed; cotton sash, not copper-threaded — sat behind it with contract parchment and a tuning fork. Across from him: a family. A man with his hands flat on the table. A woman holding the younger child's wrist the way you hold something you're afraid will be taken.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The broker struck the fork and began to sing. Cass heard the form immediately — the ratification interval, the major third, the handshake. The pitch was correct. The vowels sat in the standard positions for service-contract ratification:

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] yes, agreed, I accept these terms.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The man nodded along as though he understood the structure. He didn't. Cass could see it in his eyes — tracking the broker's mouth instead of his hands, following the performance rather than the content. The woman wasn't looking at the broker at all. She was looking at the children.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [tense] Then the under-note. It came in on the codicil — the amendment clause — threaded into the overtone the way a wire threads through wool. The surface note was clean. Underneath, the interval resolved upward. Wrong direction. The ascending resolution Torvald had shown him on the tally-strip twenty minutes ago. One hand squeezing while the other thought it shook.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The asymmetric chord gripped the family — all of them, the man and the woman and the children's futures — while the other party remained free. A lame chord, composed in real time, at a table on the Fade Line, for people who didn't know what they were hearing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass started forward. Torvald's hand closed on his arm.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [firmly] You don't have credentials. You interrupt a binding in progress without standing and they file against your whole family. Corda has another name on a contract before sundown.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stood still. The broker sang on. The man reached for the pen because the form was correct and the sound was correct and what else was he going to trust. The woman's hand tightened on the child's wrist.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald watched it happen with the expression of a man who had seen this many times and would see it again — not anger, not resignation, but the thing between them that had no name because no one who felt it had ever been in a position to stop it.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [softly] That's what it looks like. Every day.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: They watched the family sign. The broker's fork rang once more — bright, completed — and the family took their copy and stood. The man shook the broker's hand. He was smiling. The woman was not.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass turned away. The under-note sat in his jaw like a dull ache that would stay for hours.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Go home. Get some sleep.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He came back the next afternoon. Lenne was already there. She was sitting at the near end of the table with her notebook open, and she looked up when Cass came in with an expression that was not quite apologetic.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I thought another set of eyes wouldn't hurt,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said. Torvald, at the far end, didn't look up from the strip he was reading.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: She reads folk notation. Badly, but she reads it. Sit down.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass sat. He looked at Lenne. She looked back.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Your mother know you're here?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: No.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She said it without hesitation, which told him she'd thought about it and decided anyway. He let it go. The table was covered. More strips than yesterday, weighted with ledger books. The room held sound dead and close.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald had already taught Cass the basics of the notation — the deep notch for ratification, the lean of the codicils indicating direction. Cass could feel the difference now: lean left for a correct resolution, lean right for a lame chord. Lenne couldn't yet, which bothered her. She'd get there.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald moved to the far end of the table and began laying strips into groups.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Corda service contracts. I have four from the decade surrounding your brother's signing. Not the contracts themselves — the tally-songs from disputes that arose under those contracts. Wage disputes, mostly. One boundary complaint.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He tapped a group of three strips.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: This group here is interesting. The contracting party filed a codicil amendment in the second year, tried to limit their service hours. The amendment was rejected by the Corda-retained Court Singer on the grounds of improper interval.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: It wasn't improper. I had three witnesses and a licensed broker from the Potters' Quarter look at the tally-song at the time. The interval was clean. The Court Singer said it wasn't.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: What happened to the contracting party?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Still in service. Ninth year now.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He moved to the next group.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: This one resolved. The party paid out the penalty clause and left. Took them four years to save enough.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set that group aside.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: And this one —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped. Cass looked at him. Something had changed in his face — the patient precision gone. Something harder and faster, the expression of a man who has opened a drawer and found it empty when he knows he filled it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: What is it,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said. Torvald set down the strip he was holding. He moved along the table to the far end, where the shelves were, and ran his blue-green finger along a row of notches in the shelf-edge itself — a catalogue system, Cass realized, the same notch-marks as the strips, the shelf recording what should be where. Torvald ran his finger back along it. Stopped. Ran it forward again.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I have four Corda service contracts from that decade. Or I should.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He turned around. His voice was level and his face was not.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: The fourth one is gone. The strip has been pulled.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The room went quiet in a way that had nothing to do with acoustics.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Which one,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [slowly] The one from twelve years ago.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald looked at him steadily.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: The one that would cover your brother's signing year.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Nobody spoke. Lenne had stopped writing. Her pen was resting against the page and her hand wasn't moving.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: [hesitantly] Could it have been misfiled,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: No.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He said it without looking at her, still looking at Cass.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I know this archive. I built it. Every strip has a catalogue mark and a shelf position and I check them twice a year.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He moved back to the table and sat down heavily, the chair creaking under him.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That one was there six months ago. I checked it myself.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald put both hands flat on the table. Then he stood again — fast, the chair scraping stone — and went back to the shelf and ran his finger along the catalogue notches a third time, his nail catching on the wood.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [angry] Twenty years.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said. The patient measuring tone was gone. What replaced it was the voice of a craftsman looking at damage that should never have been possible.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Twenty years of records. Every dispute, every bad codicil, every contract in this district that didn't sound right. I cut every one of those strips myself.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He turned around. His face was dark.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: And someone walked into my workshop and put their hands in my shelves and pulled the one strip that would have shown what Corda did to your brother.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He picked up the nearest river-stone and set it down hard enough to shake the table. The remaining strips shifted in their rows.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: This is my house. These are my records. Whoever did this stood where you're standing and chose which piece of twenty years' work to destroy.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His hand was trembling. He pressed it flat to still it.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I want you to understand what this is. This isn't a misfiled document. This is someone reaching into my life and telling me they can take whatever they want, whenever they want, and I'll never know when it happened.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne's pen was motionless on the page.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Someone came in here,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: And they knew what to look for.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said it quiet and exact.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: You don't pull one strip from forty years of records by accident. You pull it because you know it's there and you know what it shows.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Who has access to this room?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: People I trust.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: People I trusted.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He picked up one of the remaining strips and set it down again.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: The Compact has twelve members. Three of them have been here in the last six months on business. I'm not going to tell you it wasn't one of them, because I don't know that yet.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Or someone else. Someone who knew the Compact existed and knew what you kept here and had a reason to be watching.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Or that.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Cass felt the shape of his plan — the path from Torvald's records to the Civic Court to a petition that could challenge Perin's contract — collapsing inward. Without the fourth strip, he had three contracts showing matching malformations and a gap exactly where the proof should be. He could demonstrate a pattern. He could not show the pattern touched Perin. Everything he'd built toward for two days led to a door that was no longer there.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: This changes things. I can't go to the Civic Court with three strips and an empty shelf.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: No.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The anger was still in his hands, in the set of his jaw, but he was watching Cass with that testing attention — waiting to see whether the boy would fold or turn.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Whoever took the strip knows I'm looking, and they're ahead of me. If I keep following the paper trail, they'll keep clearing it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The thought was forming as he spoke, and he let it form.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I have to come at the sound itself. Not the record of what was sung — the actual interval. If the lame chord is in Perin's contract, it's still there. You can burn a strip. You can't unring a bell.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald went still. The anger didn't leave — it was in every line of him — but something else moved behind it. Recognition, maybe.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That's a different kind of path,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: It's the only one I've got.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The three contracts you still have. Is the pattern in all of them?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald laid the remaining strips out in order. His finger moved along the notch-marks, and Cass watched his face. In each contract, the same thing: ratification clean, year-two codicil leaning wrong. The lame chord, three times running.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Same Court Singer on all three.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald turned one of the strips so Lenne could see the witness marks.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Corda-retained. Married to a Corda cousin.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Three contracts with the same malformation in the same position. And a fourth — Perin's signing year — gone.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: If the fourth one showed the same pattern,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Then it's not an accident. And someone knows it's not an accident. And they'd rather you didn't know it either.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne put her pen down. She'd been writing steadily since Torvald started laying out the strips, filling her notebook page in the small fast hand she used when she was trying to get everything down before she'd decided what mattered. Now she stopped.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Okay. So what do we do.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: We don't do anything yet. We don't have the fourth strip.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: We have three contracts showing the same illegal codicil construction.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: They're not legally usable. Torvald said so yesterday.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I said they weren't usable in a court case. That's not the same as useless.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: It's the same as not enough. If we go to a Court Singer with three folk-notation tally-songs and no fourth strip and no access to the sealed registry record —

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I'm not saying we go to a Court Singer today. I'm saying we know something now that we didn't know yesterday. That's not nothing.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: It's also not Perin.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She looked at him.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I know it's not Perin.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Then what are you suggesting we do with it? Go to the Academy? File a complaint? With what? Forty-three copper we don't have and a six-to-eight-week timeline while Corda's maintenance filing goes through and they get access to the bells?

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I'm suggesting we don't panic.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm not panicking.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: You're getting loud,

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [calm] You're getting loud,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said mildly. Cass closed his mouth. He was, in fact, getting loud.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm sorry,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said. To Lenne, not to the room.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: You're catastrophizing. That's different from thinking.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at her. She looked back, steady and precise and annoyingly correct.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Fine. What are you thinking?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She picked up her pen and turned to the next blank page in her notebook.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I'm thinking there's a pattern across three contracts. Same Court Singer, same malformation, same year-two timing. That's not an accident. That's a method.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She wrote something, showed it to him — not words, intervals, the Academy notation she'd been trained in, set next to the folk notation marks she'd been copying from the strips.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: And if it's a method, it's been used before these four contracts and it'll be used again. Which means there are other records. Not here — Torvald's already checked, and whoever pulled the fourth strip would have looked at the others too. But the disputes that arose under those contracts — some of them would have gone to the Civic Court. Public record. Not sealed.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The Civic Court records are indexed by contracting party.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: We don't have names for the contracting parties in the three strips. The tally-songs use a witness code, not a name.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Two of them resolved. I can give you names for those. The third — the one still in service — I'll need to think about whether that's mine to share.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I understand,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said, and she said it with the particular formality she used when she was taking something seriously and wanted you to know it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The fourth strip. The one that's missing. When you checked it six months ago — do you remember what it showed?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald was quiet for a moment. Outside, the work song continued, unhurried.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I remember that I thought I should look at it more carefully. I remember thinking I'd come back to it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set the strip down.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I didn't come back to it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The wrongness of it settled in his chest and stayed. Someone was ahead of him, cleaning the shelves before he arrived.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Hess,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said. Torvald looked up.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Is there anywhere else? Any other archive that might have covered the same contracts? Anyone in the Compact who was keeping their own records at the time?

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [slowly] One person,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said. He said it slowly, like he was checking the weight of it before he put it down.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: She moved to the Flood Mouth eight years ago. I haven't spoken to her since.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at the empty space on the shelf where the fourth strip should have been.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I'll write to her.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: There's also a Court Singer — independent, not Corda-retained. Name's Sorn. He's heard things too, but he won't move without evidence.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: How long will that take?

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Two weeks if she's prompt. Three if she's not.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Three weeks. The maintenance filing was already in. Three weeks was a long time to wait while Corda moved toward the bells.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: How long to learn to read these properly?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Enough to follow along? An hour.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald looked at him.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Enough to argue it before a court? Years.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I don't need a court today.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne moved her chair around to Cass's side of the table without being asked, notebook open.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Hess,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne said. She was looking at her notebook, not at either of them.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The lame chord in these contracts — does it hold the contracting party or the Corda party?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald looked at her. Something shifted in his expression, quick and considered.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That is a very good question.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Because if it holds the contracting party —

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [slowly] Then the Corda party isn't bound at all.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said. He heard his own voice come out flat and certain, the way it came out when he'd heard something he couldn't unhear.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The contract looks bilateral. It reads bilateral. The Court Singer confirms it as bilateral. But one hand is squeezing and the other hand is free.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set the strip down.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Perin is bound. Corda isn't.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The room was quiet.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: That's —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne stopped.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Yes,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: That's the whole thing. That's why it doesn't expire. A real contract has release terms that bind both parties. But if Corda isn't actually bound —

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [slowly] There's nothing to release. Because there's nothing holding them. The release would only apply to Perin, and the penalty clause for unilateral release —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped. He already knew the answer. He'd known it, he realized, since his father had said

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] your brother is well, he writes,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: in that specific flat voice. He'd just needed the vocabulary to name it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The penalty clause for unilateral release absorbs the breaching party. Which is the only party who's actually bound.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne was looking at him.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: [softly] So he can't leave,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He can't leave without triggering a penalty that finishes him. And Corda can walk away any time they like because the lame chord never gripped them.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He exhaled.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Twelve years.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Cass —

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [tense] Twelve years and the whole time the contract's been —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped himself. His jaw was tight.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Sorry. I know. We need to think.
